title: PII (Personally identifiable information)
description: Check your understanding of PII (personally identifiable information) in this set of free practice questions designed for AP Computer Science Principles students.
topic_path: Going deeper with personally identifiable information

assessment_items.question_content:
A software architect is designing the database for the Department of Motor Vehicles in their state. They need to carefully consider which information in each user account should be considered PII, so that they can better protect those fields in the database.

**Which of the following pieces of user information is _least_ likely to be considered PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Full name
Driver's license number
Phone number
Hair color

assessment_items.question_content:
A software engineer for a university is creating a system to keep track of students. They need to decide which data should be considered PII so that they can store it in a more secure database.

**Which of the following pieces of data is _most_ likely to be considered PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Declared major
GPA (Grade Point Average)
Grade level
Home address

assessment_items.question_content:
A software engineer is developing a chatbot that can discuss political issues with a human. The engineer is concerned that people might try to break into the database to discover the political opinions of the users, so they do not want to store any PII in the database at all.

**Which of these pieces of information is _least_ likely to be PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Last name
Email address
Social media username
Timezone

assessment_items.question_content:
Alani downloads a game called Kandy Krush from the app store. The app prompts her to enter her Social Security number (SSN) before playing. Alani asks her friend for advice.

**Which advice should her friend give?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: "Only enter your SSN if you're playing the game on your home wireless network; don't enter it while you're on public WiFi networks."
"The game shouldn't need your SSN to be playable; don't enter it."
"Your SSN isn't private information, so you can go ahead and enter it."
"Only enter your SSN if the app is using an encrypted connection."

assessment_items.question_content:
Online users are often asked to enter PII in online forms.

**In which of the following scenarios would it be least advisable to enter the requested PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Janelle shops at an online store and is asked to enter her credit card information at checkout.
Miriam visits a government website to renew her license and is asked to enter her drivers license number.
Bokamoso signs up for a job hunting website where she can post her résumé and is asked to add her full name in their profile.
Shi sees a posting for a product on a local marketplace, emails the poster, and is asked to reply back with her banking account information.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jairo is shopping for a new pair of shoes online and has found four online stores that sell the shoes. He opens them all up in different browser tabs and begins the checkout process so that he can find out what the final price (with shipping and taxes) will be and decide if any of the websites aren't legitimate.

**Which of these online stores seems the most suspicious?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The store that asks for his credit card number and address in the checkout form.
The store that asks for his name, phone number, credit card number, and address in the checkout form.
The store that asks for his email, age range, credit card number, and address in the checkout form.
The store that asks for his name, passport number, credit card number, and address in the checkout form.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bailey uploads a video of their neighborhood to YouTube.

**In what way could the uploaded video be used as PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The street addresses could help locate where Bailey lives.
The length of the video can help indicate how much time Bailey spends on YouTube.
The quality of the video can help indicate the type of phone Bailey has.
The size of the houses can indicate where Bailey lives.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ahmad is a graphic artist. He tweets a photo of his high school yearbook cover that he helped design.

**In what ways can the tweeted photo be used as PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The high school name indicates where Ahmad went to school.
The colors of the yearbook cover indicate Ahmad's favorite colors.
The size of the photo indicates if Ahmad has an iPhone or not.
The fonts used on the cover indicate what type of computer Ahmad used to design it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gene writes an online article for his town's local newspaper about the spread of viruses in warmer climates. 

**In what ways could this article reveal PII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The author name could be looked up in the town's property tax records to identify Gene's address.
The illustrations accompanying the article indicates Gene's favorite colors.
The topic of the article suggests that Gene is a virology expert.
The vocabulary in the article could be analyzed to estimate the educational level of the author (high school, college, graduate, etc.).

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2017, the credit report company Equifax suffered a data breach that exposed the PII of more than 100 million Americans to attackers. The exposed data included names, home addresses, phone numbers, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, and driver’s license numbers.

**With access to only that information, which of these actions could _not_ be taken by an attacker?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “Equifax Announces Cybersecurity Incident Involving Consumer Information.” 2017 Cybersecurity Incident & Important Consumer Information, September 26, 2017. https://www.equifaxsecurity2017.com/2017/09/07/equifax-announces-cybersecurity-incident-involving-consumer-information/.

choices.content: An attacker could sell the Social Security numbers to another attacker on the Internet.
An attacker could steal the identity of one of the affected people.
An attacker could post the home addresses on a public website.
An attacker could send an email to an affected person that contained their PII and a threat to reveal them.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2014, Yahoo suffered a data breach that exposed details of at least 500 million user accounts, giving attackers access to real names, email addresses, dates of birth, and telephone numbers.

**With access to that information, which of these actions could be taken by an attacker?**

👁️**Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Chappell, Bill. “Yahoo Inc. Confirms Data From 'At Least 500 Million' Users Was Stolen.” NPR. NPR, September 22, 2016. https://www.npr.org/sections/thetwo-way/2016/09/22/495056961/yahoo-inc-confirms-data-from-at-least-500-million-users-was-stolen.

choices.content: They could email a user with a threat to reveal private information.
They could send spam messages to a user's mobile phone.
They could sell the user's Social Security numbers to another attacker on the Internet.
They could post users' home addresses on a public social media account.
They could hack into a user's computer and access their file systems.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2018, the housewares company OXO discovered that attackers installed malware on their online store to steal customer information. The malware had access to the customer's name, billing and shipping addresses, and credit card information.

**With access to that information, which of the following actions could _not_ be taken by an attacker?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “Submitted Breach Notification Sample.” State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General, November 5, 2018. https://oag.ca.gov/ecrime/databreach/reports/sb24-141370.

choices.content: An attacker could login to the customer's OXO account and change account details.
An attacker could sell the customer's mailing addresses on the Internet.
An attacker could purchase items on other websites using the customer's credit card information.
An attacker could send spam mail to a customer at their home address.
